commentaries.author: Alcuin of York
commentaries.content: After speaking of the nature of the Son, he proceeds to His operations, saying, Allthings were made by him, i.e. every thing whether substance, or property.
commentaries.content_en: After speaking of the nature of the Son, he proceeds to His operations, saying, Allthings were made by him, i.e. every thing whether substance, or property.
commentaries.content_pt: Após falar sobre a natureza do Filho, ele prossegue para Suas operações, dizendo: Todas as coisas foram feitas por Ele, ou seja, tudo, seja substância ou propriedade.

commentaries.author: Alexander of Alexandria
commentaries.content: And, "by Him were all things made"
commentaries.content_en: And, "by Him were all things made"
commentaries.content_pt: E, "por Ele foram feitas todas as coisas".

commentaries.author: Augustine of Hippo
commentaries.content: Since all things were made by him, it is evident that light was as also, when God said, Let there be light. And in like manner the rest. But if so, that which God said, viz. Let there be light, is eternal. For the Word of God, God with God, is coeternal with the Father, though the world created by Him be temporal. For whereas our when and sometimes are words of time, in the Word of God, on the contrary, when a thing ought to be made, is eternal; and the thing is then made, when in that Word it is that it ought to be made, which Word has in It neither when, or at sometime, since It is all eternal. How then can the Word of God be made, when God by the Word made all things. For if the Word Itself were made, by what other Word was It made. If you say it was the Word of the Word by Which That was made, that Word I call the Only-Begotten Son of God. But if thou cost not call It the Word of the Word, then grant that that Word was not made, by which all things were made. And if It is not made, It is not a creature; but if It is not a creature, It is of the same Substance with the Father. For every substance which is not God is a creature; and what is nota creature is God. Or, by saying, without Him wasnot any thing made, he tells us not to suspect Him in any sense to be a thing made. For how can He be a thing made, when God, it is said, made nothing without Him. For sin was not made by Him; for it is manifest that sin is nothing, and that men become nothing when they sin. Nor was an idol made by the Word. It has indeed a sort of form of man, and man himself was made by the Word; but the form of man in an idol was not made by the Word: for it is written, we know that an idol is nothing. These then were not made bythe Word; but whatever things were made naturally, the whole universe, were; every creature from an angel to a worm. The folly of those men is not to be listened to, who think nothing is to be understood here as something because it is placed at the end of the sentence: as if it made so any difference whether it was said, without Him nothing was made, or, without Him was made nothing.

commentaries.content_pt: Uma vez que todas as coisas foram feitas por Ele, é evidente que a luz também foi, quando Deus disse: Haja luz. E da mesma forma o restante. Mas se assim é, aquilo que Deus disse, a saber, Haja luz, é eterno. Pois a Palavra de Deus, Deus com Deus, é coeterno com o Pai, embora o mundo criado por Ele seja temporal. Pois, enquanto nosso quando e às vezes são palavras de tempo, na Palavra de Deus, ao contrário, quando uma coisa deve ser feita, é eterna; e a coisa é então feita, quando nessa Palavra se diz que deve ser feita, a qual Palavra não tem em Si nem quando, nem em algum momento, uma vez que é toda eterna. Como, então, pode a Palavra de Deus ser feita, quando Deus, pela Palavra, fez todas as coisas? Pois se a Palavra em Si mesma fosse feita, por qual outra Palavra foi Ela feita? Se você diz que foi a Palavra da Palavra pela qual Aquela foi feita, essa Palavra eu chamo de Filho Unigênito de Deus. Mas se você não a chama de Palavra da Palavra, então conceda que essa Palavra não foi feita, pela qual todas as coisas foram feitas. E se Ela não é feita, não é uma criatura; mas se não é uma criatura, é da mesma Substância que o Pai. Pois toda substância que não é Deus é uma criatura; e o que não é uma criatura é Deus. Ou, ao dizer, sem Ele nada foi feito, ele nos diz para não suspeitar d'Ele em nenhum sentido como sendo uma coisa feita. Pois como pode Ele ser uma coisa feita, quando Deus, diz-se, não fez nada sem Ele? Pois o pecado não foi feito por Ele; pois é manifesto que o pecado não é nada, e que os homens se tornam nada quando pecam. Nem um ídolo foi feito pela Palavra. Tem, de fato, uma espécie de forma de homem, e o próprio homem foi feito pela Palavra; mas a forma de homem em um ídolo não foi feita pela Palavra: pois está escrito, sabemos que um ídolo é nada. Estes, portanto, não foram feitos pela Palavra; mas todas as coisas que foram feitas naturalmente, o universo inteiro, foram; toda criatura, de um anjo a um verme. A tolice desses homens não deve ser ouvida, que pensam que nada deve ser entendido aqui como algo porque está colocado no final da frase: como se fizesse alguma diferença se foi dito, sem Ele nada foi feito, ou, sem Ele foi feito nada.

commentaries.author: Clement Of Alexandria
commentaries.content: The divine Instructor is trustworthy, adorned as He is with three of the fairest ornament"-knowledge, benevolence, and authority of utterance;-with knowledge, for He is the paternal wisdom: "All Wisdom is from the Lord, and with Him for evermore; "-with authority of utterance, for He is God and Creator: "For all things were made by Him, and without Him was not anything made; ". He that is illuminated is therefore awake towards God; and such an one lives. "For what was made in Him was life. At home, therefore, they ought to regard with modesty parents and domestics; in the ways, those they meet; in the baths, women; in solitude, themselves; and everywhere the Word, who is everywhere, "and without Him was not anything. But a rational work is accomplished through God. "And nothing "it is said, "was made without Him"-the Word of God. And since the unoriginated Being is one, the Omnipotent God; one, too, is the First-begotten, "by whom all things were made, and without whom not one thing ever was made. "Understand now for me the mystery of the truth, granting pardon if I shrink from advancing further in the treatment of it, by announcing this alone: "All things were made by Him, and without Him was not even one thing. and if "all things were made by Him, and without Him was not anything made that was made". "by whom all things were made, and without whom not even one thing was made. (that is, of the great High Priest, "by whom all things were made, and without whom not even one thing was made". For the knowledge and apprehension of intellectual objects must necessarily be called certain scientific knowledge, whose function in reference to divine things is to consider what is the First Cause, and what that "by whom all things were made, and without whom nothing was made; ".

commentaries.content_pt: O Instrutor divino é digno de confiança, adornado como está com três dos mais belos ornamentos - conhecimento, benevolência e autoridade de enunciação; - com conhecimento, pois Ele é a sabedoria paternal: "Toda a Sabedoria é do Senhor, e com Ele para sempre;" - com autoridade de enunciação, pois Ele é Deus e Criador: "Pois todas as coisas foram feitas por Ele, e sem Ele nada foi feito;". Aquele que é iluminado está, portanto, desperto para Deus; e tal um vive. "Pois o que foi feito n'Ele era vida. Em casa, portanto, devem considerar com modéstia os pais e os domésticos; nos caminhos, aqueles que encontram; nos banhos, as mulheres; na solidão, a si mesmos; e em toda parte a Palavra, que está em toda parte, "e sem Ele nada foi feito. Mas uma obra racional é realizada através de Deus. "E nada", diz-se, "foi feito sem Ele" - a Palavra de Deus. E uma vez que o Ser não originado é um, o Deus Onipotente; um, também, é o Primogênito, "por quem todas as coisas foram feitas, e sem quem nenhuma coisa alguma foi feita." Entenda agora para mim o mistério da verdade, concedendo perdão se eu hesitar em avançar mais no tratamento disso, anunciando apenas isto: "Todas as coisas foram feitas por Ele, e sem Ele não foi feita nem mesmo uma coisa. e se "todas as coisas foram feitas por Ele, e sem Ele nada foi feito que foi feito". "por quem todas as coisas foram feitas, e sem quem nem mesmo uma coisa foi feita. (isto é, do grande Sumo Sacerdote, "por quem todas as coisas foram feitas, e sem quem nem mesmo uma coisa foi feita." Pois o conhecimento e a apreensão de objetos intelectuais devem necessariamente ser chamados de certo conhecimento científico, cuja função em referência às coisas divinas é considerar qual é a Primeira Causa, e o que é aquele "por quem todas as coisas foram feitas, e sem quem nada foi feito;".

commentaries.author: Clement Of Rome
commentaries.content:
"For in the Gospel he thus speaks: "And what was made, in Him was life, and the life was the light of men.".
He does not express the divine essence, but wishing to declare the majesty of God, he has applied to the Divinity what is best and most excellent in the view of men. Thus also Patti, when he speaks of "light inaccessible."
commentaries.content_en:
"For in the Gospel he thus speaks: "And what was made, in Him was life, and the life was the light of men.".
He does not express the divine essence, but wishing to declare the majesty of God, he has applied to the Divinity what is best and most excellent in the view of men. Thus also Patti, when he speaks of "light inaccessible."
commentaries.content_pt: "Pois no Evangelho ele assim fala: "E o que foi feito, nele estava a vida, e a vida era a luz dos homens.". Ele não expressa a essência divina, mas desejando declarar a majestade de Deus, aplicou à Divindade o que é melhor e mais excelente na visão dos homens. Assim também Patti, ao falar de "luz inacessível."

commentaries.author: Cyril of Alexandria
commentaries.content: That the Son is by Nature Creator with the Father, as being of His Essence, and not taken to Him as a minister. All things were made by Him, and without Him was not anything made. The blessed Evangelist, having overthrown the intricate objections of the unholy heretics, and having completed his subtil and most exact utterance respecting the Only-Begotten, comes to another snare of the devil compounded of the ancient deceit, and putting forth to us the sting of the polytheic error, which has wounded and cast down many, and widening the way of perdition, and throwing open the broad and spacious gate of death, heaped up souls of men in herds unto hell and set rich food as it were before the devil and brought before him choice meat. For since the children of the Greeks applying themselves to the wisdom of the world, and having plenteously in their mind the spirit of the ruler of this world, were carried away unto polytheic error, and perverted the beauty of the truth and, like to those who walk in mist and darkness, went down to the pit of their own ignorance, serving lifeless idols, and saying to a stock, Thou art my father, and to a stone, Thou hast brought me forth: others again transgressing akin to them, devising nevertheless a more polished error, deemed that they ought to worship the creature more than the Creator, and lavished the glory that befitted the Divine Nature Alone on the elements that were made by It, of necessity does the Divine introduce to us the Only-Begotten as Maker and Creator by Nature, saying that all things were made by Him and that without Him nothing passed into being, that he might close for the future the entrance for their deceits, and might shew to them that know Him |51 not the Creator of all things, and by the very words wherein he says that the creation was made, might clearly teach that other than it is He Who called it into being, and by His Ineffable Power brought things that are from not being unto birth. For thus at length was it possible by the beauty of the creatures proportionally to see the Maker, and to recognize Him Who is in truth God, through Whom all things have been already made, and made are preserved. Against the false-worship then of the Greeks do I deem that he thus well arrayed the Gospel word, and for this cause do we believe that the Only-Begotten was introduced by the voice of the saint as Maker and Creator. But since it is meet to consider the crooked inventions of the heretics, I think that we ought looking to their ways too to say again a little. All things, says he, were made by Him, and without Him was not anything made. This God-befitting dignity too does he put about the Son, on all sides shewing that He is Consubstantial with God Who begat Him and saying that all things that belong to Him by Nature are in His Offspring: that He may be conceived of as truly God of God, not (as we) having the appellation adventitious and accruing to us by grace alone, according to the words, I have said, Ye are gods and all of you are children of the most High. For if all things were made by Him, He will be Other than they all. For in this, All things, there is nothing which is not seen among all things. As the blessed Paul too is found to have understood the all things: for when in one of his Epistles he was discoursing of our Saviour and said that all things were put in subjection under His feet, excellently does he subjoin, For in that he saith all, he left nothing that is not put under Him. Therefore since we believe that all things were made by the Son, we will not think that He is one of all, but will conclude that He is external to all, and severing Him from the nature and kin of things originate, will at length confess that He is none else save God of God by Nature. For what will intervene between God and the creature. I do not mean in regard of essence, for much intervenes, but only in regard to the position of anything that is, in conception. Or what other position will the Son have, Who surpasses the nature of things made, yea rather is Himself the Maker. For all things were made "by Him, as by the Power, as by the Wisdom of God the Father, not hidden in the Nature of Him Who begat Him, as in man is for instance his innate wisdom and power, but existing separately and by Himself, yet proceeding according to the ineffable mode of Generation from the Father, that the Wisdom and Power of the Father may be conceived of as truly-existing Son. But though the blessed Evangelist says that all things were made through Him, the saying will not I deem at all minister damage to the words concerning Him. For not because it is said that the things that are were made through Him, will the Son be introduced as an underworker, or a minister of others' wills, so that He should be no longer conceived of as being by Nature Creator, nor will He be one given the power of Creation by some other, but rather being Himself Alone the Strength of God the Father, as Son, as Only-Begotten, He works all things, the Father and the Holy Ghost co-working and co-with Him: for all things are from the Father through the Son in the Holy Ghost. And we conceive of the Father as co-with the Son, not as though He were powerless to work ought of things that are, but as being wholly in Him, by reason of unchangeableness of Essence, and His entire kin and the absence of any medium towards His Natural Procession from Him. As though one were to say that to the sweet scent of a flower, the flower itself was co-present for the operation of the sweet scent, since it proceeds from it naturally. But the force of the example is slight and the Nature That is above all will overpass this too, receiving of it little-impresses of ideas. Since how shall we understand, My Father worketh hitherto and I work. For not separately and by Himself does the Son say that God the Father works ought regarding things that are, and that Himself again likewise works apart from the Father, the Essence Whence He is after some sort resting: for so the Creator would be two and not One, if Either work apart and separately. Moreover the Father will be recipient of the power of not having the Son ever in Him, and the Son likewise will be seen to not have the Father ever in Him, if it were possible that Either should work apart and separately with regard to things that are, as we said before, and the Son will not be true, when He says, I am in the Father and the Father in Me. For it is not, I suppose, merely after likeness of Essence, that we see the Son in the Father as Express Image, or again the Father in the Son as Archetype; but we hold that the Son beams forth by Generation from the Essence of the Father, and is and subsists in It and of It in distinct Being, God the Word: and that the Father again is in the Son, as in Consubstantial Offspring, Connaturally, yet severally, according to simply the difference of being, and being conceived of as that which He is. For the Father remains that which He is, even though He be Connaturally in the Son, as we say that the Sun is in its brightness. And the Son again will be conceived of, as not other than He is, even if He be Connaturally in the Father, as in the sun its brightness. For thus, the Father being conceived of and being in truth Father, the Son again being and conceived of as Son, the Holy Ghost having His place with them, the number of the Holy Trinity mounts to One and the Same Godhead. For how will God be at all conceived of as One, if Each of the Persons mentioned withdraw into a complete individuality, and, while wholly removed from Connature and Essential participation with the Other, be called God. Therefore let us conceive of Father, Son and Spirit, according to the mode of individual being, not mixing up the difference of the Persons or names in regard to That Which Each IS: but while we reserve severally to each the being and being called what He IS, and thus believe, referring them still of Nature to One Godhead, and refusing to hold a complete severance, because the Son is called the Word and Wisdom and Brightness and Express Image and Might of the Father. For He is Word and Wisdom, by reason of these being, immediately and without any intervention, of the mind and in the mind, and because of the reciprocal interpassing into one another so to say of both. For the mind is seen in word and wisdom, and word in its turn in the mind, and there is nought that intervenes, or severs the one from the other. He is called Power again, as being a quality inherent without any interval in those who have it, and that can nowise be severed from them in the manner of an accident, apart from the destruction of the subject: Express Image again, as being even connate, and unable to be severed from the essence of which it is the express image. Hence since Either is naturally and of necessity in Other, when the Father works the Son will work, as being His Natural and Essential and Hypostatic Power. Likewise when the Son works, the Father too works, as the Source, of the Creating Word, Naturally In-existent in His Own Offspring, even as the fire too in the heat that proceeds from it. It is clear then, that vainly has been iterated the accusation of the opponents against the Only-Begotten, who introduce Him to us as creator by having learnt, yea rather as minister too; because of the Blessed Evangelist saying, All things were made through Him and without Him, was not anything made. Much do I marvel at the unholy heretics: for whatever seems any way to undo the Dignity of the Only-Begotten and to shew Him second to Him Who begat Him, according to their own view, this they hunt with much zeal, and from all sides bring to it the drugs of their own stubbornness; whatever again are healthfully and rightly said and bring the Son up to the Glory of the Father, these things they bury most surely in deep silence, as having one sole aim, to in vain revile Him Who is glorified of all the creation. For when they hear that All things were made through Him, they hotly bring on Him the name of service, dreaming that the Son is bond instead of free, and worshipper rather than Lord. But when they learn that without Him was not anything made, they do not mount up to think ought great and marvellous of Him. For since it is not in God the Father to create otherwise than by His own Offspring, Which is His Wisdom |55 and Power, the Evangelist says that nought at all was made without Him. For therefore is the Only-Begotten the Glory of God the Father (for He is glorified as Creator through the Son); for He worketh all things and bringeth into being things that are not. And well will one conceive of the words, without Him, was not anything made, if he consider with himself what was said at the creation of man. For Let us make man, says he, in Our image after Our likeness. For here specially one can behold in the Son of a truth nought that is lowly, as in a minister according to their phrase. For God the Father does not command the Word, Make man, but as Co-with Him by Nature and His inseparably so to say In-existing Co-worker, He made Him also Partaker of His Counsel respecting man, not anticipating the knowledge that is in the Son in regard to any conception, but as Mind inseparably and apart from time manifested in the in-imaged and in-existing Word. Let God-befitting contemplations again be above the reach of the example. Yet we say that He co-works with the Son, not conceiving as of two severally, lest there be conceived to be two gods, nor yet as though both together were one, in order that neither the Son be compressed into Father, nor again the Father into Son, but rather in such sort as if one allowed to be co-existent in the brightness from light the light whence it flashed forth: for in such examples the generator seems to be separated in idea from the generated and that which springs forth from it indivisibly; yet are both one and the same by nature, and the one in no wise separate from the other. But above this too will God again be, inasmuch as He is both Super-substantial and has nothing wholly like Him in things originate, that it should be taken as a image of the Holy Trinity, without any difference, in exactness of doctrine. But if they deem that the word, through Whom, said of the Son, can bring down His Essence from Equality and Natural likeness to the Father, so as to be minister rather than Creator, let those insane consider and come forward and make answer, what we are to conceive of the Father Himself also, and Whom we are to suppose Him too to be, seeing that He clearly receives the words through Whom in the Divine Scripture: for God, says he, is faithful, through Whom ye were called unto the fellowship of His Son, and Paul an Apostle of Jesus Christ through the will of God: and again Paul writeth to some, Wherefore thou art no more a servant but a son; and if a son, then an heir through God. All these then have reference to the Person of God the Father, and no one I suppose will rush to that extreme of madness (except perchance he hold with the above mentioned), as to say that the name and fact of service, is reasonably predicated of the very glory of the Father, because the word through Whom is applied to Him too. For the Divine Scripture is sometimes indifferent in regard to its words, in no wise wronging the subject thereby, but applying to the things signified in a less proper sense both the words themselves and those whereby it deems that they are well explained. But it is well to say of those, that The glory of the Lord veileth speech. For little in truth is all might of words unto the exact exposition of the Ineffable and God-befitting glory. Wherefore one must not be offended at the meanness of the things uttered, but must rather yield supremacy, and might in tongue, and keenness of every mind, to the Divine and unutterable Nature, for thus shall we be and not in small degree pious.

For what will intervene between God and the creature. I do not mean in regard of essence, for much intervenes, but only in regard to the position of anything that is, in conception. Or what other position will the Son have, Who surpasses the nature of things made, yea rather is Himself the Maker. For all things were made "by Him, as by the Power, as by the Wisdom of God the Father, not hidden in the Nature of Him Who begat Him, as in man is for instance his innate wisdom and power, but existing separately and by Himself, yet proceeding according to the ineffable mode of Generation from the Father, that the Wisdom and Power of the Father may be conceived of as truly-existing Son. But though the blessed Evangelist says that all things were made through Him, the saying will not I deem at all minister damage to the words concerning Him. For not because it is said that the things that are were made through Him, will the Son be introduced as an underworker, or a minister of others' wills, so that He should be no longer conceived of as being by Nature Creator, nor will He be one given the power of Creation by some other, but rather being Himself Alone the Strength of God the Father, as Son, as Only-Begotten, He works all things, the Father and the Holy Ghost co-working and co-with Him: for all things are from the Father through the Son in the Holy Ghost. And we conceive of the Father as co-with the Son, not as though He were powerless to work ought of things that are, but as being wholly in Him, by reason of unchangeableness of Essence, and His entire kin and the absence of any medium towards His Natural Procession from Him. As though one were to say that to the sweet scent of a flower, the flower itself was co-present for the operation of the sweet scent, since it proceeds from it naturally. But the force of the example is slight and the Nature That is above all will overpass this too, receiving of it little-impresses of ideas. Since how shall we understand, My Father worketh hitherto and I work. For not separately and by Himself does the Son say that God the Father works ought regarding things that are, and that Himself again likewise works apart from the Father, the Essence Whence He is after some sort resting: for so the Creator would be two and not One, if Either work apart and separately. Moreover the Father will be recipient of the power of not having the Son ever in Him, and the Son likewise will be seen to not have the Father ever in Him, if it were possible that Either should work apart and separately with regard to things that are, as we said before, and the Son will not be true, when He says, I am in the Father and the Father in Me. For it is not, I suppose, merely after likeness of Essence, that we see the Son in the Father as Express Image, or again the Father in the Son as Archetype; but we hold that the Son beams forth by Generation from the Essence of the Father, and is and subsists in It and of It in distinct Being, God the Word: and that the Father again is in the Son, as in Consubstantial Offspring, Connaturally, yet severally, according to simply the difference of being, and being conceived of as that which He is. For the Father remains that which He is, even though He be Connaturally in the Son, as we say that the Sun is in its brightness. And the Son again will be conceived of, as not other than He is, even if He be Connaturally in the Father, as in the sun its brightness. For thus, the Father being conceived of and being in truth Father, the Son again being and conceived of as Son, the Holy Ghost having His place with them, the number of the Holy Trinity mounts to One and the Same Godhead. For how will God be at all conceived of as One, if Each of the Persons mentioned withdraw into a complete individuality, and, while wholly removed from Connature and Essential participation with the Other, be called God. Therefore let us conceive of Father, Son and Spirit, according to the mode of individual being, not mixing up the difference of the Persons or names in regard to That Which Each IS: but while we reserve severally to each the being and being called what He IS, and thus believe, referring them still of Nature to One Godhead, and refusing to hold a complete severance, because the Son is called the Word and Wisdom and Brightness and Express Image and Might of the Father. For He is Word and Wisdom, by reason of these being, immediately and without any intervention, of the mind and in the mind, and because of the reciprocal interpassing into one another so to say of both. For the mind is seen in word and wisdom, and word in its turn in the mind, and there is nought that intervenes, or severs the one from the other. He is called Power again, as being a quality inherent without any interval in those who have it, and that can nowise be severed from them in the manner of an accident, apart from the destruction of the subject: Express Image again, as being even connate, and unable to be severed from the essence of which it is the express image. Hence since Either is naturally and of necessity in Other, when the Father works the Son will work, as being His Natural and Essential and Hypostatic Power. Likewise when the Son works, the Father too works, as the Source, of the Creating Word, Naturally In-existent in His Own Offspring, even as the fire too in the heat that proceeds from it. It is clear then, that vainly has been iterated the accusation of the opponents against the Only-Begotten, who introduce Him to us as creator by having learnt, yea rather as minister too; because of the Blessed Evangelist saying, All things were made through Him and without Him, was not anything made. Much do I marvel at the unholy heretics: for whatever seems any way to undo the Dignity of the Only-Begotten and to shew Him second to Him Who begat Him, according to their own view, this they hunt with much zeal, and from all sides bring to it the drugs of their own stubbornness; whatever again are healthfully and rightly said and bring the Son up to the Glory of the Father, these things they bury most surely in deep silence, as having one sole aim, to in vain revile Him Who is glorified of all the creation. For when they hear that All things were made through Him, they hotly bring on Him the name of service, dreaming that the Son is bond instead of free, and worshipper rather than Lord. But when they learn that without Him was not anything made, they do not mount up to think ought great and marvellous of Him. For since it is not in God the Father to create otherwise than by His own Offspring, Which is His Wisdom |55 and Power, the Evangelist says that nought at all was made without Him. For therefore is the Only-Begotten the Glory of God the Father (for He is glorified as Creator through the Son); for He worketh all things and bringeth into being things that are not. And well will one conceive of the words, without Him, was not anything made, if he consider with himself what was said at the creation of man. For Let us make man, says he, in Our image after Our likeness. For here specially one can behold in the Son of a truth nought that is lowly, as in a minister according to their phrase. For God the Father does not command the Word, Make man, but as Co-with Him by Nature and His inseparably so to say In-existing Co-worker, He made Him also Partaker of His Counsel respecting man, not anticipating the knowledge that is in the Son in regard to any conception, but as Mind inseparably and apart from time manifested in the in-imaged and in-existing Word. Let God-befitting contemplations again be above the reach of the example. Yet we say that He co-works with the Son, not conceiving as of two severally, lest there be conceived to be two gods, nor yet as though both together were one, in order that neither the Son be compressed into Father, nor again the Father into Son, but rather in such sort as if one allowed to be co-existent in the brightness from light the light whence it flashed forth: for in such examples the generator seems to be separated in idea from the generated and that which springs forth from it indivisibly; yet are both one and the same by nature, and the one in no wise separate from the other. But above this too will God again be, inasmuch as He is both Super-substantial and has nothing wholly like Him in things originate, that it should be taken as a image of the Holy Trinity, without any difference, in exactness of doctrine. But if they deem that the word, through Whom, said of the Son, can bring down His Essence from Equality and Natural likeness to the Father, so as to be minister rather than Creator, let those insane consider and come forward and make answer, what we are to conceive of the Father Himself also, and Whom we are to suppose Him too to be, seeing that He clearly receives the words through Whom in the Divine Scripture: for God, says he, is faithful, through Whom ye were called unto the fellowship of His Son, and Paul an Apostle of Jesus Christ through the will of God: and again Paul writeth to some, Wherefore thou art no more a servant but a son; and if a son, then an heir through God. All these then have reference to the Person of God the Father, and no one I suppose will rush to that extreme of madness (except perchance he hold with the above mentioned), as to say that the name and fact of service, is reasonably predicated of the very glory of the Father, because the word through Whom is applied to Him too. For the Divine Scripture is sometimes indifferent in regard to its words, in no wise wronging the subject thereby, but applying to the things signified in a less proper sense both the words themselves and those whereby it deems that they are well explained. But it is well to say of those, that The glory of the Lord veileth speech. For little in truth is all might of words unto the exact exposition of the Ineffable and God-befitting glory. Wherefore one must not be offended at the meanness of the things uttered, but must rather yield supremacy, and might in tongue, and keenness of every mind, to the Divine and unutterable Nature, for thus shall we be and not in small degree pious.
commentaries.content_pt: Que o Filho é por Natureza Criador com o Pai, sendo da Sua Essência, e não tomado para Ele como um ministro. Todas as coisas foram feitas por Ele, e sem Ele nada foi feito. O bendito Evangelista, tendo derrubado as intrincadas objeções dos hereges ímpios, e tendo completado sua expressão sutil e mais exata a respeito do Unigênito, chega a outra armadilha do diabo composta da antiga enganação, e apresentando a nós o veneno do erro politeísta, que feriu e derrubou muitos, alargando o caminho da perdição e abrindo a ampla e espaçosa porta da morte, amontoou almas de homens em rebanhos para o inferno e colocou diante do diabo como que uma rica comida e trouxe diante dele carne escolhida. Pois, uma vez que os filhos dos gregos se aplicaram à sabedoria do mundo, e tendo abundantemente em suas mentes o espírito do governante deste mundo, foram levados ao erro politeísta, e perverteram a beleza da verdade e, como aqueles que andam em névoa e escuridão, desceram ao abismo de sua própria ignorância, servindo ídolos sem vida, e dizendo a um tronco: Tu és meu pai, e a uma pedra: Tu me geraste: outros, novamente transgredindo semelhantes a eles, elaborando, no entanto, um erro mais polido, consideraram que deveriam adorar a criatura mais do que o Criador, e lavraram a glória que convinha à Natureza Divina Sozinha nos elementos que foram feitos por Ela, de necessidade a Divina nos apresenta o Unigênito como Criador e Criador por Natureza, dizendo que todas as coisas foram feitas por Ele e que sem Ele nada passou a existir, para que Ele pudesse fechar para o futuro a entrada para seus enganos, e pudesse mostrar a eles que não conhecem a Ele |51 não o Criador de todas as coisas, e pelas próprias palavras nas quais ele diz que a criação foi feita, possa ensinar claramente que outro é Ele que a chamou à existência, e por Seu Poder Inefável trouxe as coisas que são do não ser ao nascimento. Pois assim, ao fim, era possível pela beleza das criaturas ver proporcionalmente o Criador, e reconhecer Aquele que é verdadeiramente Deus, por meio de Quem todas as coisas já foram feitas, e as coisas feitas são preservadas. Contra o falso culto então dos gregos, considero que ele assim bem arranjou a palavra do Evangelho, e por esta causa acreditamos que o Unigênito foi apresentado pela voz do santo como Criador e Criador. Mas uma vez que é conveniente considerar as invenções tortuosas dos hereges, penso que devemos, olhando para seus caminhos também, dizer novamente um pouco. Todas as coisas, diz ele, foram feitas por Ele, e sem Ele nada foi feito. Esta dignidade que convém a Deus também ele coloca sobre o Filho, mostrando de todos os lados que Ele é Consubstancial com Deus que O gerou e dizendo que todas as coisas que pertencem a Ele por Natureza estão em Sua Prole: para que Ele possa ser concebido como verdadeiramente Deus de Deus, não (como nós) tendo a denominação adventícia e que nos é atribuída apenas pela graça, de acordo com as palavras: Eu disse: Vós sois deuses e todos vós sois filhos do Altíssimo. Pois se todas as coisas foram feitas por Ele, Ele será Outro que todas elas. Pois neste, Todas as coisas, não há nada que não seja visto entre todas as coisas. Como o bendito Paulo também se mostra ter entendido todas as coisas: pois quando em uma de suas Epístolas ele estava discursando sobre nosso Salvador e disse que todas as coisas foram colocadas em sujeição sob Seus pés, ele acrescenta excelentemente: Pois ao dizer todas, ele não deixou nada que não esteja sob Ele. Portanto, uma vez que acreditamos que todas as coisas foram feitas pelo Filho, não pensaremos que Ele é um entre todos, mas concluiremos que Ele é externo a todos, e separando-O da natureza e parentesco das coisas originadas, confessaremos finalmente que Ele não é outro senão Deus de Deus por Natureza. Pois o que intervirá entre Deus e a criatura. Não me refiro à essência, pois muito intervém, mas apenas em relação à posição de qualquer coisa que é, em concepção. Ou que outra posição terá o Filho, que supera a natureza das coisas feitas, sim, antes é Ele mesmo o Criador. Pois todas as coisas foram feitas "por Ele, como pela Força, como pela Sabedoria de Deus o Pai, não escondida na Natureza d'Aquele que O gerou, como no homem é, por exemplo, sua sabedoria e poder inatos, mas existindo separadamente e por Si mesmo, ainda que procedendo de acordo com o modo inefável de Geração do Pai, para que a Sabedoria e o Poder do Pai possam ser concebidos como Filho verdadeiramente existente. Mas embora o bendito Evangelista diga que todas as coisas foram feitas através d'Ele, a afirmação não considero de modo algum que prejudique as palavras a respeito d'Ele. Pois não porque se diz que as coisas que são foram feitas através d'Ele, será o Filho apresentado como um trabalhador subordinado, ou um ministro das vontades de outros, de modo que Ele não seja mais concebido como sendo por Natureza Criador, nem será um dado o poder da Criação por algum outro, mas sim, sendo Ele Mesmo Sozinho a Força de Deus o Pai, como Filho, como Unigênito, Ele faz todas as coisas, o Pai e o Espírito Santo cooperando e co-trabalhando com Ele: pois todas as coisas são do Pai através do Filho no Espírito Santo. E concebemos o Pai como co-com o Filho, não como se Ele fosse impotente para realizar algo das coisas que são, mas como estando totalmente n'Ele, em razão da imutabilidade da Essência, e Sua inteira parentela e a ausência de qualquer meio em relação à Sua Processão Natural d'Ele. Como se alguém dissesse que ao doce perfume de uma flor, a própria flor estivesse co-presente para a operação do doce perfume, uma vez que procede dela naturalmente. Mas a força do exemplo é leve e a Natureza Que está acima de tudo superará isso também, recebendo dela pequenas impressões de ideias. Pois como devemos entender, Meu Pai trabalha até agora e Eu trabalho. Pois não separadamente e por Si mesmo diz o Filho que Deus o Pai trabalha algo em relação às coisas que são, e que Ele mesmo novamente igualmente trabalha à parte do Pai, a Essência de Onde Ele é, de certa forma repousando: pois assim o Criador seria dois e não Um, se Ambos trabalhassem à parte e separadamente. Além disso, o Pai será receptor do poder de não ter o Filho sempre n'Ele, e o Filho igualmente será visto como não ter o Pai sempre n'Ele, se fosse possível que Ambos trabalhassem à parte e separadamente em relação às coisas que são, como dissemos antes, e o Filho não será verdadeiro, quando diz: Eu estou no Pai e o Pai em Mim. Pois não é, suponho, meramente segundo a semelhança da Essência, que vemos o Filho no Pai como Imagem Expressa, ou novamente o Pai no Filho como Arquetipo; mas sustentamos que o Filho irradia por Geração da Essência do Pai, e é e subsiste n'Ele e d'Ele em Ser distinto, Deus o Verbo: e que o Pai novamente está no Filho, como em Prole Consubstancial, Connaturalmente, ainda que separadamente, de acordo simplesmente com a diferença de ser, e sendo concebido como aquilo que Ele é. Pois o Pai permanece aquilo que Ele é, mesmo que Ele esteja Connaturalmente no Filho, como dizemos que o Sol está em seu brilho. E o Filho novamente será concebido como não sendo outro senão Ele é, mesmo que Ele esteja Connaturalmente no Pai, como no brilho do sol. Pois assim, o Pai sendo concebido e sendo verdadeiramente Pai, o Filho novamente sendo e concebido como Filho, o Espírito Santo tendo Seu lugar com eles, o número da Santa Trindade sobe a Um e o Mesmo Deus. Pois como Deus será concebido como Um, se Cada uma das Pessoas mencionadas se retirar para uma individualidade completa, e, enquanto totalmente afastadas da Connatureza e participação Essencial com o Outro, sejam chamadas de Deus. Portanto, devemos conceber o Pai, o Filho e o Espírito, de acordo com o modo de ser individual, não misturando a diferença das Pessoas ou nomes em relação àquilo que Cada Um É: mas enquanto reservamos separadamente a cada um o ser e ser chamado o que Ele É, e assim acreditamos, referindo-os ainda por Natureza a Uma só Divindade, e recusando manter uma separação completa, porque o Filho é chamado de Verbo e Sabedoria e Brilho e Imagem Expressa e Poder do Pai. Pois Ele é Verbo e Sabedoria, em razão de serem estes, imediatamente e sem qualquer intervenção, da mente e na mente, e por causa da interpenetração recíproca um no outro, assim dizer, de ambos. Pois a mente é vista na palavra e na sabedoria, e a palavra, por sua vez, na mente, e não há nada que intervenha, ou separe um do outro. Ele é chamado Poder novamente, como sendo uma qualidade inerente sem qualquer intervalo naqueles que a têm, e que não pode de modo algum ser separada deles na maneira de um acidente, à parte da destruição do sujeito: Imagem Expressa novamente, como sendo até mesmo connatural, e incapaz de ser separada da essência da qual é a imagem expressa. Daí, uma vez que Ambos estão naturalmente e de necessidade no Outro, quando o Pai trabalha, o Filho trabalhará, como sendo Seu Poder Natural, Essencial e Hipostático. Da mesma forma, quando o Filho trabalha, o Pai também trabalha, como a Fonte, da Palavra Criadora, Existente Naturalmente em Sua Própria Prole, assim como o fogo também no calor que dele procede. Está claro então, que em vão foi reiterada a acusação dos oponentes contra o Unigênito, que O introduzem a nós como criador por terem aprendido, sim, antes como ministro também; por causa do Bendito Evangelista dizer: Todas as coisas foram feitas através d'Ele e sem Ele nada foi feito. Muito me maravilho com os hereges ímpios: pois tudo o que parece de alguma forma desfazer a Dignidade do Unigênito e mostrá-Lo segundo Aquele que O gerou, segundo sua própria visão, isso eles perseguem com muito zelo, e de todos os lados trazem a ele as drogas de sua própria teimosia; o que novamente é dito de forma saudável e correta e eleva o Filho à Glória do Pai, essas coisas eles enterram com certeza em profundo silêncio, como tendo um único objetivo, em vão difamar Aquele que é glorificado por toda a criação. Pois quando ouvem que Todas as coisas foram feitas através d'Ele, eles rapidamente lhe atribuem o nome de serviço, sonhando que o Filho é escravo em vez de livre, e adorador em vez de Senhor. Mas quando aprendem que sem Ele nada foi feito, eles não se elevam para pensar algo grande e maravilhoso a Seu respeito. Pois uma vez que não é próprio de Deus o Pai criar de outra forma senão por Seu próprio Filho, Que é Sua Sabedoria |55 e Poder, o Evangelista diz que nada foi feito sem Ele. Pois por isso o Unigênito é a Glória de Deus o Pai (pois Ele é glorificado como Criador através do Filho); pois Ele faz todas as coisas e traz à existência coisas que não são. E bem se pode conceber as palavras, sem Ele, nada foi feito, se considerar consigo mesmo o que foi dito na criação do homem. Pois Façamos o homem, diz ele, à Nossa imagem conforme a Nossa semelhança. Pois aqui especialmente pode-se ver no Filho de verdade nada que seja humilde, como em um ministro segundo a sua frase. Pois Deus o Pai não ordena ao Verbo: Faça o homem, mas como Co-com Ele por Natureza e Seu inseparavelmente, assim dizer, Existente Co-trabalhador, Ele fez também Parte do Seu Conselho a respeito do homem, não antecipando o conhecimento que está no Filho em relação a qualquer concepção, mas como Mente inseparavelmente e à parte do tempo manifestada na Palavra imaginada e existente. Que contemplações que convêm a Deus estejam acima do alcance do exemplo. No entanto, dizemos que Ele co-trabalha com o Filho, não concebendo como se fossem dois separadamente, para que não se conceba haver dois deuses, nem como se ambos juntos fossem um, de modo que nem o Filho seja comprimido no Pai, nem novamente o Pai no Filho, mas sim de tal maneira como se alguém permitisse ser co-existente no brilho da luz a luz da qual ela irrompeu: pois em tais exemplos o gerador parece ser separado em ideia do gerado e daquilo que brota dele indivisivelmente; no entanto, ambos são um e o mesmo por natureza, e um de forma alguma separado do outro. Mas acima disso também estará Deus novamente, na medida em que Ele é tanto Super-substancial e não tem nada totalmente semelhante a Ele nas coisas originadas, que deveria ser tomado como uma imagem da Santa Trindade, sem qualquer diferença, na exatidão da doutrina. Mas se eles consideram que a palavra, através da qual, dita do Filho, pode rebaixar Sua Essência da Igualdade e Semelhança Natural ao Pai, de modo a ser ministro em vez de Criador, que aqueles insanos considerem e avancem e respondam, o que devemos conceber do próprio Pai também, e a quem devemos supor que Ele seja, uma vez que Ele claramente recebe as palavras através das quais na Escritura Divina: pois Deus, diz ele, é fiel, através do qual fostes chamados à comunhão de Seu Filho, e Paulo um Apóstolo de Jesus Cristo através da vontade de Deus: e novamente Paulo escreve a alguns: Por isso já não és mais servo, mas filho; e se filho, então herdeiro através de Deus. Todas essas coisas então têm referência à Pessoa de Deus o Pai, e ninguém, suponho, se precipitará a esse extremo de loucura (exceto talvez quem concorde com os mencionados acima), a ponto de dizer que o nome e o fato de serviço, é razoavelmente predicado da própria glória do Pai, porque a palavra através da qual é aplicada a Ele também. Pois a Escritura Divina é às vezes indiferente em relação às suas palavras, de modo algum prejudicando o sujeito por isso, mas aplicando às coisas significadas em um sentido menos próprio tanto as palavras em si quanto aquelas pelas quais considera que estão bem explicadas. Mas é bom dizer sobre essas coisas, que A glória do Senhor vela a fala. Pois pouco, na verdade, é todo o poder das palavras para a exata exposição da Inefável e da glória que convém a Deus. Portanto, não se deve ofender com a mesquinhez das coisas proferidas, mas deve-se antes ceder a supremacia, e poder na língua, e agudeza de toda mente, à Natureza Divina e inefável, pois assim seremos e não em pequena medida piedosos.

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: All things were made by him, and without him was made nothing that was made. These words teach us, that all created being, visible, or invisible on earth, every thing that ever was made, or began to be, were made, produced, and created by this eternal word, or by the Son of God. The same is truly said of the Holy Spirit; all creatures being equally produced, created, and preserved by the three divine Persons as, by their proper, principal, and efficient cause, in the same manner, and by the same action: not by the Son, in any manner inferior to the Father; nor as if the Son produced things only ministerially, and acted only as the minister, and instrument of the Father, as the Arians pretended. In this sublime mystery of one God and three distinct Persons, if we consider the eternal processions, and personal proprieties, the Father is the first Person, but not by any priority of time, or of dignity; all the three divine Persons being eternal, or co-eternal, equal in all perfections, being one in nature, in substance, in power, in majesty: in a word, one and the same God. The Father in no other sense is called the first Person, but because he proceeds from none, or from no other person: and the eternal Son is the second Person begotten, and proceeding from him, the Father, from all eternity, proceeds now, and shall proceed from him for all eternity; as we believe that the third divine Person, the Holy Spirit, always proceeded without any beginning, doth now proceed, and shall proceed for ever, both from the Father and the Son. But when we consider and speak of any creatures, of any thing that was made, or had a beginning, all things were equally created in time, and are equally preserved, no less by the Son, and by the Holy Spirit, than by the Father. For this reason St. John tells us again in this chapter, (ver. ) that the world was made by the word. And our Saviour himself (John v. ) tells us, that whatsoever the Father doth, these things also in like manner, or in the same manner, the Son doth. Again the apostle, (Hebrews i. ) speaking of the Son, says, the world was made by him: and in the same chapter, (ver. ) he applies to the Son these words, (Psalm ci. ) And thou, O Lord, in the beginning didst found the earth: and the heavens are the works of thy hands To omit other places, St. Paul again, writing to the Colossians, (Chap. ) and speaking of God's beloved Son, as may be seen in that chapter, says, that in him all things were created, visible and invisible all things were created in him, and by him, or, as it is in the Greek, unto him, and for him; to show that the Son was not only the efficient cause, the Maker and Creator of all things, but also the last end of all. Which is also confirmed by the following words: And he is before all, and all things subsist in him, or consist in him; as in the Rheims and Protestant translations. I have, therefore, in this third verse, translated, all things were made by him, with all English translations and paraphrases, whether made by Catholics or Protestants; and not all things were made through him, lest through should seem to carry with it a different and a diminishing signification; or as if, in the creation of the world, the eternal word, or the Son of God, produced things only ministerially, and, in a manner, inferior to the Father, as the Arians and Eunomians pretended; against whom, on this very account, wrote St. de spiritu Sto. Chrysostom, and St. Cyril, on this very verse; where they expressly undertake to show that the Greek text in this verse no ways favours these heretics. The Arians, and now the Socinians, who deny the Son to be true God, or that the word God applies as properly to him as to the Father, but would have him called God, that is, a nominal god, in an inferior and improper sense; as when Moses called the god of Pharao; (Exodus vii. ) or as men in authority are called gods; (Psalm lxxxi. ) pretend, after Origen, to find another difference in the Greek text; as if, when mention is made of the Father, he is styled the God; but that the Son is only called God, or a God. This objection St. Chrysostom, St. Cyril, and others, have shown to be groundless: that pretended significant Greek article being several times omitted, when the word God is applied to God the Father; and being found in other places, when the Son of God is called God. See this objection fully and clearly answered by the author of a short book, published in the year 1729, against Dr. Clark and Mr. Whist on, p. 64, and seq. (Witham) Were made Mauduit here represents the word: "1. As a cause, or principle, acting extraneously from himself upon the void space, in order to give a being to all creatures: "whereas there was no void space before the creation. Ante omnia Deus erat solus, ipse sibi et mundus et locus, et omnia. (Tertullian, lib. Augustine in Psalm cxxii. says: antequam face ret Deus Sanctos, ubi habitabat. In se habitabat, apud se habitabat. The creation of all things, visible and invisible, was the work of the whole blessed Trinity; but the Scriptures generally attribute it to the word; because wisdom, reason, and intelligence, which are the attributes of the Son, are displayed most in it. (Calmet) What wonderful tergiversations the Arians used to avoid the evidence of this text, we see in St. Augustine, lib. 2; even such as modern dissenters do, to avoid the evidence of This is my Body, concerning the blessed Eucharist.
commentaries.content_en: All things were made by him, and without him was made nothing that was made. These words teach us, that all created being, visible, or invisible on earth, every thing that ever was made, or began to be, were made, produced, and created by this eternal word, or by the Son of God. The same is truly said of the Holy Spirit; all creatures being equally produced, created, and preserved by the three divine Persons as, by their proper, principal, and efficient cause, in the same manner, and by the same action: not by the Son, in any manner inferior to the Father; nor as if the Son produced things only ministerially, and acted only as the minister, and instrument of the Father, as the Arians pretended. In this sublime mystery of one God and three distinct Persons, if we consider the eternal processions, and personal proprieties, the Father is the first Person, but not by any priority of time, or of dignity; all the three divine Persons being eternal, or co-eternal, equal in all perfections, being one in nature, in substance, in power, in majesty: in a word, one and the same God. The Father in no other sense is called the first Person, but because he proceeds from none, or from no other person: and the eternal Son is the second Person begotten, and proceeding from him, the Father, from all eternity, proceeds now, and shall proceed from him for all eternity; as we believe that the third divine Person, the Holy Spirit, always proceeded without any beginning, doth now proceed, and shall proceed for ever, both from the Father and the Son. But when we consider and speak of any creatures, of any thing that was made, or had a beginning, all things were equally created in time, and are equally preserved, no less by the Son, and by the Holy Spirit, than by the Father. For this reason St. John tells us again in this chapter, (ver. ) that the world was made by the word. And our Saviour himself (John v. ) tells us, that whatsoever the Father doth, these things also in like manner, or in the same manner, the Son doth. Again the apostle, (Hebrews i. ) speaking of the Son, says, the world was made by him: and in the same chapter, (ver. ) he applies to the Son these words, (Psalm ci. ) And thou, O Lord, in the beginning didst found the earth: and the heavens are the works of thy hands To omit other places, St. Paul again, writing to the Colossians, (Chap. ) and speaking of God's beloved Son, as may be seen in that chapter, says, that in him all things were created, visible and invisible all things were created in him, and by him, or, as it is in the Greek, unto him, and for him; to show that the Son was not only the efficient cause, the Maker and Creator of all things, but also the last end of all. Which is also confirmed by the following words: And he is before all, and all things subsist in him, or consist in him; as in the Rheims and Protestant translations. I have, therefore, in this third verse, translated, all things were made by him, with all English translations and paraphrases, whether made by Catholics or Protestants; and not all things were made through him, lest through should seem to carry with it a different and a diminishing signification; or as if, in the creation of the world, the eternal word, or the Son of God, produced things only ministerially, and, in a manner, inferior to the Father, as the Arians and Eunomians pretended; against whom, on this very account, wrote St. de spiritu Sto. Chrysostom, and St. Cyril, on this very verse; where they expressly undertake to show that the Greek text in this verse no ways favours these heretics. The Arians, and now the Socinians, who deny the Son to be true God, or that the word God applies as properly to him as to the Father, but would have him called God, that is, a nominal god, in an inferior and improper sense; as when Moses called the god of Pharao; (Exodus vii. ) or as men in authority are called gods; (Psalm lxxxi. ) pretend, after Origen, to find another difference in the Greek text; as if, when mention is made of the Father, he is styled the God; but that the Son is only called God, or a God. This objection St. Chrysostom, St. Cyril, and others, have shown to be groundless: that pretended significant Greek article being several times omitted, when the word God is applied to God the Father; and being found in other places, when the Son of God is called God. See this objection fully and clearly answered by the author of a short book, published in the year 1729, against Dr. Clark and Mr. Whist on, p. 64, and seq. (Witham) Were made Mauduit here represents the word: "1. As a cause, or principle, acting extraneously from himself upon the void space, in order to give a being to all creatures: "whereas there was no void space before the creation. Ante omnia Deus erat solus, ipse sibi et mundus et locus, et omnia. (Tertullian, lib. Augustine in Psalm cxxii. says: antequam face ret Deus Sanctos, ubi habitabat. In se habitabat, apud se habitabat. The creation of all things, visible and invisible, was the work of the whole blessed Trinity; but the Scriptures generally attribute it to the word; because wisdom, reason, and intelligence, which are the attributes of the Son, are displayed most in it. (Calmet) What wonderful tergiversations the Arians used to avoid the evidence of this text, we see in St. Augustine, lib. 2; even such as modern dissenters do, to avoid the evidence of This is my Body, concerning the blessed Eucharist.
commentaries.content_pt: Todas as coisas foram feitas por ele, e sem ele nada foi feito do que foi feito. Essas palavras nos ensinam que todo ser criado, visível ou invisível na terra, tudo o que foi feito ou começou a ser, foi feito, produzido e criado por esta palavra eterna, ou pelo Filho de Deus. O mesmo é verdadeiramente dito do Espírito Santo; todas as criaturas sendo igualmente produzidas, criadas e preservadas pelas três Pessoas divinas como, por sua causa própria, principal e eficiente, da mesma maneira, e pela mesma ação: não pelo Filho, de maneira alguma inferior ao Pai; nem como se o Filho produzisse as coisas apenas ministerialmente, e agisse apenas como o ministro e instrumento do Pai, como os arianos pretendiam. Neste sublime mistério de um Deus e três Pessoas distintas, se considerarmos as processões eternas e as propriedades pessoais, o Pai é a primeira Pessoa, mas não por qualquer prioridade de tempo ou dignidade; todas as três Pessoas divinas sendo eternas, ou coeternas, iguais em todas as perfeições, sendo uma na natureza, na substância, no poder, na majestade: em uma palavra, um e o mesmo Deus. O Pai de nenhuma outra maneira é chamado a primeira Pessoa, mas porque Ele não procede de ninguém, ou de nenhuma outra pessoa: e o eterno Filho é a segunda Pessoa gerada, e procedente d'Ele, o Pai, de toda a eternidade, procede agora, e procederá d'Ele por toda a eternidade; assim como acreditamos que a terceira Pessoa divina, o Espírito Santo, sempre procedeu sem qualquer começo, procede agora, e procederá para sempre, tanto do Pai quanto do Filho. Mas quando consideramos e falamos de qualquer criatura, de qualquer coisa que foi feita ou teve um começo, todas as coisas foram igualmente criadas no tempo, e são igualmente preservadas, não menos pelo Filho, e pelo Espírito Santo, do que pelo Pai. Por esta razão, São João nos diz novamente neste capítulo, (ver.) que o mundo foi feito pela palavra. E nosso Salvador mesmo (João v.) nos diz que tudo o que o Pai faz, essas coisas também de maneira semelhante, ou da mesma maneira, o Filho faz. Novamente o apóstolo, (Hebreus i.) falando do Filho, diz que o mundo foi feito por ele: e no mesmo capítulo, (ver.) ele aplica ao Filho estas palavras, (Salmo ci.) E tu, ó Senhor, no princípio fundaste a terra: e os céus são as obras de tuas mãos. Para omitir outros lugares, São Paulo novamente, escrevendo aos colossenses, (Cap.) e falando do Filho amado de Deus, como pode ser visto naquele capítulo, diz que nele todas as coisas foram criadas, visíveis e invisíveis, todas as coisas foram criadas nele, e por ele, ou, como está em grego, para ele, e por ele; para mostrar que o Filho não era apenas a causa eficiente, o Criador e Criador de todas as coisas, mas também o último fim de todas. O que também é confirmado pelas palavras seguintes: E ele está antes de todas as coisas, e todas as coisas subsistem nele, ou consistem nele; como nas traduções de Rheims e Protestante. Portanto, eu traduzi, neste terceiro versículo, todas as coisas foram feitas por ele, com todas as traduções e paráfrases em inglês, sejam feitas por católicos ou protestantes; e não todas as coisas foram feitas através dele, para que através parecesse carregar consigo um significado diferente e diminuído; ou como se, na criação do mundo, a palavra eterna, ou o Filho de Deus, produzisse as coisas apenas ministerialmente, e, de certa forma, inferior ao Pai, como os arianos e eunomianos pretendiam; contra os quais, por esta mesma razão, escreveu São João Crisóstomo, e São Cirilo, sobre este mesmo versículo; onde eles se comprometem expressamente a mostrar que o texto grego neste versículo de modo algum favorece esses hereges. Os arianos, e agora os socinianos, que negam que o Filho seja verdadeiro Deus, ou que a palavra Deus se aplica tão propriamente a ele quanto ao Pai, mas gostariam que ele fosse chamado Deus, isto é, um deus nominal, em um sentido inferior e impróprio; como quando Moisés chamou o deus de Faraó; (Êxodo vii.) ou como homens em autoridade são chamados de deuses; (Salmo lxxxi.) pretendem, após Orígenes, encontrar outra diferença no texto grego; como se, quando se menciona o Pai, ele é chamado o Deus; mas que o Filho é apenas chamado Deus, ou um Deus. Esta objeção São João Crisóstomo, São Cirilo, e outros, mostraram ser infundada: que o pretendido artigo significativo grego sendo várias vezes omitido, quando a palavra Deus é aplicada a Deus o Pai; e sendo encontrado em outros lugares, quando o Filho de Deus é chamado Deus. Veja esta objeção plenamente e claramente respondida pelo autor de um pequeno livro, publicado no ano de 1729, contra o Dr. Clark e o Sr. Whiston, p. 64, e seg. (Witham) Foram feitas Mauduit aqui representa a palavra: "1. Como uma causa, ou princípio, agindo externamente a partir de si mesmo sobre o espaço vazio, a fim de dar ser a todas as criaturas: "enquanto não havia espaço vazio antes da criação. Ante omnia Deus erat solus, ipse sibi et mundus et locus, et omnia. (Tertuliano, lib. Agostinho no Salmo cxxii. diz: antequam face ret Deus Sanctos, ubi habitabat. In se habitabat, apud se habitabat. A criação de todas as coisas, visíveis e invisíveis, foi obra de toda a bendita Trindade; mas as Escrituras geralmente a atribuem à palavra; porque a sabedoria, razão e inteligência, que são os atributos do Filho, se manifestam mais nela. (Calmet) Que maravilhosas tergiversações os arianos costumavam usar para evitar a evidência deste texto, vemos em Santo Agostinho, lib. 2; mesmo como os dissidentes modernos fazem, para evitar a evidência de Isto é meu Corpo, a respeito da bendita Eucaristia.

commentaries.author: Hilary of Poitiers
commentaries.content:
Or thus:, the Word indeed was in the beginning, but it may be that He was not before the beginning. But what says he; All things were made by him. He is infinite by Whom everything, which is, was made: and since all things were made by Him, time is likewise.
Or thus; That all things were made by him, is pronouncing too much, it maybe said. There is an Unbegotten Who is made of none, and there is the Son Himself begotten from Him Who is Unbegotten. The Evangelist however again implies the Author, when he speaks of Him as Associated; saying, without Him was not any thing made. This, that nothing was made without Him, I understand to mean the Son’s not being alone, for ‘by whom’ is one thing, ‘not without whom another.

commentaries.content_pt:
Ou assim: o Verbo de fato estava no princípio, mas pode ser que Ele não estivesse antes do princípio. Mas o que diz ele; Todas as coisas foram feitas por ele. Ele é infinito por Quem tudo, que é, foi feito: e uma vez que todas as coisas foram feitas por Ele, o tempo também é.
Ou assim; Que todas as coisas foram feitas por ele, é pronunciar demais, pode-se dizer. Há um Não Gerado que não é feito de ninguém, e há o próprio Filho gerado d'Aquele que é Não Gerado. O Evangelista, no entanto, novamente implica o Autor, quando fala d'Ele como Associado; dizendo, sem Ele nada foi feito. Isso, que nada foi feito sem Ele, entendo significar que o Filho não está sozinho, pois 'por quem' é uma coisa, 'não sem quem' é outra.

commentaries.author: Hippolytus of Rome
commentaries.content:
"For all things "he says, "were made by him, and not even one thing was made without him, and what was made in him is life.".
"In this way, he says, the Phrygians call him "Amygdalus "from which proceeded and was born the Invisible (One), "by whom all things were made, and nothing was made without Him."

for the Word was the author of form and beginning to all the Aeons that came into existence after Him. But "what was made in Him "says John, "is life.".
